subject: Physics
title: The Calorie: Measuring Heat
description: A quick win: understand The Calorie: Measuring Heat.

hook.text: The calorie on food labels is actually a kilocalorie. One food Calorie heats one liter of water by 1°C.

buildup.text: The calorie was defined in 1824 as the energy needed to heat one gram of water by one degree Celsius.

discovery.text: Food energy is measured by burning food in a calorimeter and measuring the temperature rise of surrounding water.

twist.text: A single peanut contains enough energy to heat a liter of water by about 5°C when burned.

climax.text: Scientists now prefer joules—the SI unit. One food Calorie equals 4,186 joules of energy.

punchline.text: Burn food. Heat water. Measure the rise. That's a calorie.

quiz.question: How is the energy content of food measured?
options: By burning it and measuring the heat released
By weighing it on a precise scale
